summary: This paper introduces VisionED, a framework for organic-material property prediction that represents the electron density (ED) of a molecule as a small set of multi-shot 2D images rendered from DFT calculations, rather than as an expensive volumetric field or ED point cloud. The authors assemble a corpus of roughly two million molecules, render on the order of twelve million multi-shot ED images, and define a 69-dimensional set of quantum descriptors (ESSOR). A ViT-Base encoder is pre-trained with three physics-informed pretext tasks - atomic charge prediction, ESSOR regression, and quantum semantic clustering - and then fine-tuned on downstream organic-material benchmarks spanning photovoltaic (HOPV15) and organic-chromophore (OPEP2, Deep4Chem) properties. The model is evaluated under scaffold splits, out-of-distribution transfer, and low-data regimes, and compared against geometric graph networks, ED point-cloud methods, and image-based baselines. The paper further reports an efficiency comparison showing large memory and time savings relative to ED point clouds, and demonstrates an application to donor-acceptor virtual screening and ternary-blend property recovery.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The problem this paper targets is both important and timely. Learning from a physics-rich but computationally expensive modality such as the electron density once, and then amortizing that signal across scarce experimental materials tasks, is an attractive design pattern for AI-for-science, and the paper executes a concrete and practical instance of it. Representing the ED as a compact set of rendered views instead of a full volumetric field or point cloud is a simple, scalable idea, and the reported efficiency gains over the ED point-cloud representation are concrete and meaningful - for example, the six-shot configuration reaches a substantially lower PCE MAE at roughly a third of the GPU memory of the best point-cloud setting. The empirical study is also more thorough than average: it spans several downstream datasets, includes low-data and cross-dataset/OOD settings, and provides ablations over both the pretraining objectives and the view strategy. The ablation over pretext tasks is particularly well-structured, showing that atomic charge prediction, ESSOR regression, and quantum semantic clustering each contribute on their own and combine for the best result.

My main reservation is that several of the headline claims are not as firmly supported as the text implies. A number of the reported gains over the strongest baselines are within a single standard deviation (for instance the VOC improvement under the acceptor split), yet no significance tests are reported anywhere, so it is difficult to tell which margins are reliable. The device-scale ternary-blend result, on which the abstract's high accuracy figure rests, is drawn from only five systems with no confidence intervals and an accuracy defined against single experimental measurements; as presented this is closer to an anecdote than a defensible statistical claim. Most importantly, the central physics-informed pre-training argument is not properly isolated: the without-pre-training baseline is a randomly initialized ViT, whereas the right counterfactual is an ImageNet-pretrained ViT fine-tuned on the same tasks. Without that control it is impossible to say how much of the lift comes specifically from ED-informed pre-training versus any strong pretrained vision backbone.

A related concern is that the rendering appears to entangle ED geometry with electrostatic-potential-style coloring, so it is unclear how much of the benefit derives from the electron density itself as opposed to ESP-like surface cues. Ablations that isolate these signals - grayscale ED-only, ESP-only, and geometry-only renderings - would considerably strengthen the physics-informed narrative. On the physical side, projecting a continuous 3D ED field onto a finite set of 2D views is inherently lossy, and the coupling between some target properties and the ground-state ED is not fully established; several photovoltaic properties depend on excited-state or electron-phonon effects that ground-state DFT densities do not directly encode.

The theoretical treatment of rotation invariance is looser than it appears. The appendix argument is formally correct under i.i.d. Haar sampling of views, but the implementation uses a fixed set of canonical views; the Wasserstein-discrepancy patch is never evaluated numerically for the small number of views actually used, and the empirical gap between canonical and Fibonacci sampling is well within run-to-run variance, so it neither validates nor refutes the theory. The claim would be more convincing if the bound were instantiated for the deployed configuration.

The cost and motivation also deserve a more careful framing. Obtaining the ED still requires a DFT calculation, so the data-scarcity motivation is somewhat misleading - any DFT run already produces the density, and the real difficulty is its storage and representation cost rather than its availability. Given that the end-to-end pipeline still relies on DFT to generate its inputs, the improvements over cheaper non-ED baselines such as EquiformerV2 are not always large enough to make the added electronic-structure cost obviously worthwhile, and this trade-off should be quantified.

Finally, the positioning and terminology could be tightened. VideoMol is used as an experimental baseline but is not discussed as prior art for multi-view molecular image rendering, which it directly is; engaging it properly would let the paper frame its genuine novelty more precisely - efficient ED-image rendering plus large-scale ED pretraining for organic-material tasks - rather than as the first ED-aware or pretrained molecular model. The self-supervised label is also imprecise, since two of the three pretext tasks are automatic supervision from DFT-derived targets rather than label-free objectives. The description of the data pipeline (whether densities were computed anew or taken from an existing bank), the fairness of comparing against baselines that were not pretrained on the same corpus, and whether the pretraining corpus overlaps with the downstream benchmarks are all points that should be clarified. Overall the presentation is clear and the experimental scope is commendable, and the underlying idea is sound and useful; the paper would be considerably stronger with the missing controls, statistical rigor, and more precise claims.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. What is the downstream performance of an ImageNet-pretrained ViT fine-tuned on OPEP2, HOPV15, and Deep4Chem? The current without-pre-training baseline is randomly initialized, so this control is needed to isolate how much lift comes specifically from ED-informed pre-training versus any strong pretrained backbone.
2. Can you disentangle the contribution of ED geometry from the electrostatic-potential coloring - for example with grayscale ED-only, ESP-only, and geometry-only renderings - so that the physics-informed claim can be attributed to the electron density itself?
3. For how many metrics do the gains over the best baseline exceed one pooled standard deviation, and can pairwise significance tests be provided? Relatedly, can the ternary-blend evaluation be expanded well beyond five systems with reported confidence intervals so that the accuracy claim can stand on its own?
4. Has the distributional shift between the B3LYP/6-31G** densities used for pre-training and the MMFF94/GFN2-xTB conformers used for fine-tuning been characterized, and is there any overlap or near-overlap between the pretraining corpus and the downstream benchmarks?
5. What is the estimated true end-to-end inference cost on unseen candidates, including the DFT step needed to generate the ED inputs, and how does this trade off against cheaper non-ED baselines given the observed margins?
limitations: The limitations section is largely reasonable, but it omits a meaningful practical point: the protocol/conformer-quality gap between the DFT densities used for pre-training and the semi-empirically generated structures used for fine-tuning is never surfaced in the main text and should be. The paper's continued reliance on DFT to produce the ED inputs at inference time could also be stated more directly as a limitation, since it bears on when the method is worth its added cost.
